ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese stir-fried corn (Bap Xao). Mapped to the broader FoodOn class 'stir-fried food product' (FOODON_00002809) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Maize, sweet corn — used as the primary ingredient anchor for this stir-fried corn dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for Vietnamese stir-fried corn. A0CKP (sweet corn) is used as the primary ingredient anchor. The composite dish nature (butter, dried shrimp, spring onion) is documented in classification.main_ingredients.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Bap Xao or Vietnamese stir-fried corn as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Bap Xao is approximately 250 g (one plate), providing around 380 kcal, suitable as a side dish or light snack portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Corn, sweet, yellow, cooked (FDC ID 170108); Butter, salted (FDC ID 173410); Shrimp, dried (FDC ID 175180) — composite ingredient estimation for stir-fried corn dish
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Bap Xao exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked sweet corn (FDC ID 170108), butter (FDC ID 173410), and dried shrimp (FDC ID 175180), weighted by typical recipe proportions (~65% corn, ~10% butter, ~8% dried shrimp, ~17% spring onion and seasoning). Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7, source: grabfood) were cross-checked against this USDA composite estimate and found broadly consistent; USDA-derived values are used as the authoritative basis. Sodium at 300 mg/100g is plausible given fish sauce and dried shrimp content.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — sweet corn analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bap Xao as a composite dish. GI of 60 is estimated from sweet corn GI values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283 (sweet corn boiled: GI range 52–60, mean ~56; rounded to 60 to account for added sugar). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (60 × 15.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 22.8 per 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100g from fish sauce and dried shrimp; estimated 750 mg sodium per standard 250 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Bap Xao to small portions and request reduced fish sauce and dried shrimp, as a 250 g serving delivers approximately 750 mg sodium — one-third of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~60) and moderate carbohydrate load (15.2 g/100g; GL ~22.8 per serving); dietary fibre at 1.6 g/100g is below the 3 g/100g threshold for meaningful glucose modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Bap Xao in controlled portions (100–150 g), pair it with lower-GI foods, and request reduced added sugar, as the medium glycaemic index and moderate glycaemic load per serving can raise postprandial blood glucose if eaten in large quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.0 g/100g from butter and trans fat 0.2 g/100g; sodium 300 mg/100g; cholesterol 32 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, Bap Xao should be consumed occasionally and in small portions; substituting butter with extra-virgin olive oil would reduce saturated fat and trans fat intake, and requesting less fish sauce would lower the sodium contribution per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Butter-derived saturated fat (4.0 g/100g) is pro-inflammatory; no meaningful anti-inflammatory factors present (fibre 1.6 g/100g, no omega-3, minimal vitamin C at 4 mg/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute butter with extra-virgin olive oil and add ginger or turmeric to introduce anti-inflammatory polyphenols, as the current formulation lacks any positive anti-inflammatory nutritional factor.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bap Xao (Vietnamese: Bắp Xào) is a popular Vietnamese stir-fried corn dish widely sold at street food stalls and casual restaurants across Vietnam and Singapore. The dish is prepared by stir-frying sweet corn kernels in butter over high heat, then adding dried shrimp (tôm khô), sliced spring onions, and a savoury seasoning sauce typically made from fish sauce, salt, and a small amount of sugar. The result is a dish with a sweet, slightly smoky, and umami-rich flavour profile, with tender corn kernels coated in a glossy, buttery sauce and punctuated by the chewy texture of rehydrated dried shrimp. Per 100 g, Bap Xao provides approximately 152 kcal, 4.8 g protein, 8.0 g fat (of which 4.0 g is saturated fat from butter), 15.2 g carbohydrate, 6.0 g sugar, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The medium glycaemic index (~60) and moderate glycaemic load (~22.8 per 250 g serving) make portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp is a concern for hypertension management. Clinically, the dish is best consumed in small portions by individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. Replacing butter with olive oil and reducing fish sauce are practical modifications to improve the nutritional profile.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Vietnamese stir-fried corn?
Is Bap Xao suitable for diabetics?
Is Bap Xao high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Bap Xao exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked sweet corn, butter, and dried shrimp, weighted by typical recipe proportions. The input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7, source: grabfood) were cross-checked and found broadly consistent with the USDA composite estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are USDA FoodData Central composite estimates adapted from sweet corn (FDC 170108), butter (FDC 173410), and dried shrimp (FDC 175180); no exact approved-source entry for Bap Xao exists. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese stir-fried corn dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo as a single composite entry as of 2026. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.5) are estimated from USDA butter and corn fatty acid profiles. 4. potassium_mg (200) is estimated from USDA sweet corn data. 5. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database sweet corn values; no direct GI measurement for Bap Xao exists. 6. Halal status is marked false because dried shrimp sourcing and fish sauce brand halal certification cannot be confirmed from available data; consumers should verify with the vendor. 7. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 4.8, fat_g 8.0, carbohydrate_g 15.2) are non-zero. 8. Chinese local names (simplified and traditional: 越南炒玉米), Malay (Jagung Goreng Vietnam), Tamil (வியட்நாம் வதக்கிய சோளம்), and Hindi (वियतनामी भुट्टा स्टर-फ्राई) added to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance; this dish is sold in Singapore and the Singaporean multilingual naming rule applies in addition to the Vietnamese requirement. 9. The dish is primarily Vietnamese cuisine but is commonly served in Singapore; both Vietnamese (native-script) and Singaporean multilingual naming requirements are applied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 152.0, protein_g 4.8, fat_g 8.0, carbohydrate_g 15.2 all present and numeric. nutrient_source cites USDA FoodData Central (approved source) with adapted composite methodology fully documented. Forbidden source (grabfood) not used as primary evidence.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 1.6, sodium_mg 300.0, saturated_fat_g 4.0, sugar_g 6.0 all present as numeric values and plausible.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 60, gi_category medium, glycemic_load_per_serving 22.8, gi_source citing University of Sydney GI Database (approved GI source) with analogous estimate basis fully documented including peer-reviewed journal reference.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002809 (stir-fried food product) used as broader ancestor with gap note. FoodEx2 A0CKP (sweet corn) used as primary ingredient anchor with adaptation note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented. Match types set to closest_parent.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is over 1,200 characters, covers what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: All strings from aliases_by_language verified present in flat aliases array: 越南炒玉米 (chinese), Jagung Goreng Vietnam (malay), வியட்நாம் வதக்கிய சோளம் (tamil), वियतनामी भुट्टा स्टर-फ्राई (hindi), Bắp Xào and bắp xào tôm khô (vietnamese), Bap Xao, bap xao tom kho, corn xao (romanized), Vietnamese stir-fried corn, Vietnamese corn stir-fry, stir-fried sweet corn Vietnamese (english). Consistency verified. Mandatory language alias coverage complete.
